problem_addressed: The paper addresses the challenge of human-robot interaction where robots must anticipate human intent in ambiguous scenarios, particularly focusing on the problem that deep learning models for intent prediction are prone to being confidently incorrect.
core_claim: The paper presents Risk-Calibrated Interactive Planning (RCIP), a framework that statistically calibrates risk for uncertain action selection in human-robot cooperation, allowing robots to meet user-specified risk levels with specified probability over new scenarios.
key_contributions: Development of Risk-Calibrated Interactive Planning (RCIP), a framework for measuring and calibrating risk in uncertain action selection for HRI
Statistical risk calibration method that allows robots to meet user-specified risk levels with specified probability over new scenarios
A practical approach that enables flexible autonomy while controlling multiple risks simultaneously (action miscoverage and human help rate)
novelty: Unlike traditional conformal prediction which only allows choosing prediction thresholds to bound miscoverage rates, RCIP permits modification of other model parameters (like temperature of softmax outputs) for more fine-grained control of prediction sets. It also introduces the concept of controlling multiple risks simultaneously rather than just focusing on prediction accuracy.
method_summary: The RCIP framework uses a calibration dataset to select parameter pairs (prediction thresholds λ and model parameters θ) that control user-specified risk levels. It employs a multiple-hypothesis testing approach using fixed-sequence testing to identify parameter sets that control the family-wise error rate across different risk metrics. The method generates prediction sets of actions and triggers human help when the set contains multiple actions.
method_components: Prediction Model: Uses models like Vision Language Models (VLMs) to predict intent probabilities
Intent-Conditioned Planner: Generates actions conditioned on predicted intents
Risk Calibration Module: Uses statistical methods to select parameters that control risk
Help Trigger Mechanism: Requests human assistance when prediction sets contain multiple actions
key_innovation: The key innovation is the use of multiple-hypothesis testing with fixed-sequence testing to simultaneously control different risk metrics (action miscoverage and human help rate) by selecting appropriate prediction thresholds and model parameters, rather than just focusing on prediction accuracy.
assumptions: The robot has access to a calibration dataset drawn from the same distribution as future scenarios
The robot has ground-truth knowledge of the true intent z when available
The risk signal can be properly defined and measured
The prediction model produces softmax scores for each intent-conditioned action
theoretical_grounding: The method is grounded in the Learn-then-Test framework for distribution-free statistical risk control. It uses the Hoeffding-Bentkus inequality to bound the probability of suboptimal actions and employs fixed-sequence testing to control the family-wise error rate. Propositions 1 and 2 provide theoretical bounds on action miscoverage rate and human help rate respectively.
compute_required: Habitat simulator, Franka Emika Panda arm, Azure Kinect RGB-D camera
headline_result: RCIP achieves 100% plan success and 100% step success with 0% help in Social Navigation (Model 3)
ablations: Effect of action miscoverage (αcov) and help rate risk limits (αhelp) on FWER-controlling parameter set size
improvement_over_baseline: RCIP reduces plan-wise help rate by 11% and step-wise help rate by 5% in both Open-Category Sorting and Bimanual Sorting compared to baselines
best_result: RCIP achieves 100% plan success and 100% step success with 0% help in Social Navigation (Model 3)
title: Risk-Calibrated Human-Robot Interaction via Set-Valued Intent Prediction
